benchmark.noiseDesc: Context noise dilutes the model's attention window. Removing it helps the model stay grounded in actual code structure and reduces the chance of hallucination.
benchmark.signalDesc: 10K tokens of focused context outperform 200K of boilerplate. The model spends its attention on logic, not on JSDoc comments and import boilerplate.
benchmark.stat1Desc: Tree-sitter parses every file. The AST skeleton stays intact — functions, classes, types, and their relationships are never lost.
benchmark.stat2Desc: Function names, variable names, type annotations, and import paths are preserved. The LLM can still reference any symbol by name.
benchmark.stat3Desc: Meaningful code lines are retained. Only boilerplate, redundant comments, and low-entropy patterns are compressed away.
benchmark.step1Desc: Pass a single file, a directory, or a glob pattern. The benchmark engine processes everything it finds.
benchmark.step1Title: Point at any file or directory
benchmark.step2Desc: Uses tiktoken with the o200k_base encoding (same as GPT-4o, Claude, and modern LLMs). No estimates — real token counts.
benchmark.step3Desc: Get accuracy scores and savings percentages for every compression mode. Pick the right mode for each use case.
benchmark.whyDesc1: LLMs have a fixed attention budget. Every token in the context window competes for attention weights. Filling the window with boilerplate means less attention on the code that matters.
benchmark.whyDesc2: By removing noise before it reaches the model, lean-ctx increases the information density of every request. The result: higher signal-to-noise ratio, less context dilution, and the model stays within useful context limits.
blog.mcpOverhead.broaderLi1: Compressed tool representations that convey semantics in fewer tokens
blog.mcpOverhead.broaderLi2: Tool consolidation that reduces the number of definitions
blog.mcpOverhead.broaderLi3: Context-aware loading that only sends relevant tools per request
blog.mcpOverhead.broaderLi4: Protocol-level optimization that eliminates redundancy at the wire format
blog.mcpOverhead.broaderP1: This isn't just a lean-ctx problem. Every MCP server — GitHub, database tools, cloud providers — faces the same overhead. A typical development environment with 3-4 MCP servers might consume 50,000+ tokens just in tool definitions.
blog.mcpOverhead.broaderP2: The AI-code communication layer needs a new paradigm. Instead of verbose tool schemas, we need:
blog.mcpOverhead.broaderP3: This is what lean-ctx is building: not just a compression tool, but an context engineering layer that makes AI-code communication fundamentally more efficient.
blog.mcpOverhead.description: We measured the actual token cost of Model Context Protocol tool definitions in Cursor. The results are alarming: 72% of your context window is consumed before you even ask a question.
blog.mcpOverhead.heroMeta: March 29, 2026 · 6 min read · by Yves Gugger
blog.mcpOverhead.heroTitle:
MCP Wastes 22,945 Tokens
Before Your First Message
blog.mcpOverhead.introP1: We measured the actual token overhead of Model Context Protocol (MCP) tool definitions in a real Cursor session. The results fundamentally challenge the assumption that MCP is efficient for AI-code communication.
blog.mcpOverhead.measurementP1: A standard lean-ctx MCP server exposes 21 tools. Each tool definition includes a name, description, and JSON schema for parameters. We measured the token count of these definitions using tiktoken-rs (the same tokenizer OpenAI uses).
blog.mcpOverhead.measurementP2: That's 22,945 tokens before the user types a single character. On Claude 3.5 Sonnet at $3/M input tokens, that's $0.069 per message — just for telling the model which tools exist. Over a typical 8-hour coding session with 200 tool calls, that's $13.80 in pure overhead.
blog.mcpOverhead.solutionP1: lean-ctx v2.8.2 introduces Unified Tool Mode — consolidating 21 individual tools into 4 core tools + 1 meta-tool:
blog.mcpOverhead.solutionP2: The meta-tool (ctx) acts as a router: ctx(tool="session", action="load") replaces the dedicated ctx_session tool. The 4 high-frequency tools (ctx_read, ctx_shell, ctx_search, ctx_tree) keep their own definitions for zero-overhead access.
blog.mcpOverhead.stat3Label: per message just for tool overhead (GPT-4)

docs.intelligenceLayer.boltzmannIntro: Once Spectral Relevance selects the relevant files, each file needs a token budget. Naive approaches give equal budgets or sort by relevance. Boltzmann Allocation uses statistical mechanics to distribute tokens optimally.
docs.intelligenceLayer.boltzmannStep1: Each file has a relevance score Ei from Spectral Relevance.
docs.intelligenceLayer.boltzmannStep2: A temperature parameter β is derived from the task: specific tasks ("fix auth bug in login.ts") produce low temperature (sharp allocation to top files); broad tasks ("refactor the codebase") produce high temperature (even distribution).
docs.intelligenceLayer.boltzmannStep4: Minimum and maximum budgets are enforced (128–4096 tokens per file), and the compression mode (full, map, signatures) is chosen based on the allocated budget.
docs.intelligenceLayer.bpeHowItWorks: Each rule is a simple string replacement applied line-by-line after all other compression stages. Rules are derived from BPE token boundary analysis — they target patterns where the tokenizer produces unnecessarily many tokens for semantically equivalent text.
docs.intelligenceLayer.bpeImpact: BPE optimization typically saves 3–8% additional tokens on already-compressed output. The savings compound across files and are most significant for verbose languages (TypeScript, Java, C#).
docs.intelligenceLayer.bpeIntro: After all content is selected and ordered, a final pass optimizes the raw text for the LLM's BPE tokenizer. Small formatting changes can significantly reduce token count without changing semantics.
docs.intelligenceLayer.chunkingIntro: LLMs suffer from the "Lost in the Middle" problem: information at the beginning and end of context receives more attention than content in the middle. Semantic Chunking restructures output to minimize information loss.
docs.intelligenceLayer.chunkingStep1: Source lines are grouped into semantic chunks (functions, types, imports, loose logic) based on AST boundary heuristics.
docs.intelligenceLayer.chunkingStep2: Chunks matching the current task are promoted to the top (high-attention position). Remaining chunks are ordered by type priority.
docs.intelligenceLayer.chunkingStep3: Between chunks, lean-ctx inserts minimal bridge markers (---) so the LLM recognizes structural boundaries.
docs.intelligenceLayer.chunkingStep4: The last 2–3 lines of the highest-priority chunk are repeated at the very end of the output, exploiting the recency bias for critical information.
docs.intelligenceLayer.description: Six compression algorithms grounded in information theory, graph theory, and statistical mechanics — all running automatically inside lean-ctx.
docs.intelligenceLayer.intro1: lean-ctx goes beyond pattern matching. Six algorithms from information theory, graph theory, and statistical mechanics work together to decide what to keep, what to cut, and where to put it — all automatically, all locally, zero configuration.
docs.intelligenceLayer.intro2: These algorithms power the autonomous intelligence layer. They activate during every ctx_read, ctx_preload, and ctx_overview call — you never invoke them directly.
docs.intelligenceLayer.mmrImpact: In a typical multi-file preload, MMR removes 10–25% of redundant content — primarily shared imports and repeated utility patterns.
docs.intelligenceLayer.mmrIntro: When multiple files are loaded into context, they often contain duplicate imports, shared boilerplate, or very similar code patterns. Maximum Marginal Relevance (MMR) removes this redundancy while preserving unique information.
docs.intelligenceLayer.mmrStep1: For each line, compute bigram Jaccard similarity against all previously selected lines (the "coverage set").
docs.intelligenceLayer.mmrStep2: The MMR score balances relevance against redundancy:
docs.intelligenceLayer.mmrStep3: Lines with MMR < 0 are suppressed — they add more redundancy than information. The λ parameter defaults to 0.7 (favoring relevance over diversity).
docs.intelligenceLayer.rowBpeDecides: How to encode the final text

docs.intelligenceLayer.spectralFormula: The final relevance score combines both: 0.7 × heat + 0.3 × pagerank. Files below a threshold are excluded from preloading.
docs.intelligenceLayer.spectralGraphIntr: Two graph algorithms run on the project's dependency graph:
docs.intelligenceLayer.spectralHeatDesc: Seed files matching the task description receive initial "heat". Heat spreads along import edges with exponential decay, simulating information flow through the codebase.
docs.intelligenceLayer.spectralIntro: When you ask lean-ctx to preload context for a task, it needs to decide which files matter. Simple keyword matching only finds files that mention your search terms. Spectral Relevance finds files that are structurally connected to the relevant ones — even if they share no keywords.
docs.intelligenceLayer.spectralPageRankD: Files imported by many other files receive higher centrality scores. This identifies structural hubs (e.g., a db.ts imported by 20 modules).
docs.intelligenceLayer.spectralZeroConfi: The dependency graph builds automatically on first use via load_or_build(). No ctx_graph build needed — it just works.
docs.intelligenceLayer.surpriseImpact: Predictive Surprise removes 15–30% more boilerplate than character-level entropy while preserving complex logic. It directly models what the LLM considers "informative" because it uses the same BPE vocabulary.
docs.intelligenceLayer.surpriseIntro: Traditional compression filters use Shannon entropy on raw characters, which treats aaaa and import as equally predictable. Predictive Surprise instead measures cross-entropy against BPE token frequency — how surprising each line is to the LLM's tokenizer.
docs.intelligenceLayer.surpriseStep1: Each line is tokenized using o200k_base (GPT-4o / Claude tokenizer).
docs.intelligenceLayer.surpriseStep2: For each token, a Zipfian prior estimates expected frequency based on rank.
docs.intelligenceLayer.surpriseStep3: The cross-entropy is computed: lines with common tokens (boilerplate) score low; lines with rare tokens (complex logic) score high.
docs.intelligenceLayer.surpriseStep4: Lines below the surprise threshold are candidates for removal during aggressive compression.

docs.treeSitter.howItWorksDesc: When you use ctx_read with --mode signatures or --mode map:
docs.treeSitter.howStep1: LeanCTX detects the file extension and loads the corresponding tree-sitter grammar.
docs.treeSitter.howStep2: The source code is parsed into an AST in a single pass.
docs.treeSitter.howStep3: Pre-compiled SCM queries match definition nodes (functions, classes, structs, etc.).
docs.treeSitter.howStep4: Each match is converted into a compact Signature object with name, parameters, return type, visibility, and async status.
docs.treeSitter.howStep5: The signatures are formatted using compact notation (or TDD notation if enabled).
docs.treeSitter.intro: Since v1.5.0, LeanCTX uses tree-sitter for signature extraction instead of line-by-line regex matching. Tree-sitter parses source code into a full Abstract Syntax Tree (AST), enabling accurate extraction of multi-line signatures, arrow functions, decorators, and nested definitions that regex cannot handle.

docsCliReference.shellAliasesDesc: After running lean-ctx init --global, these aliases are available in your shell. They let you toggle compression on and off without restarting:
docsCliReference.shellAliasesWhenToDisab: When to disable: Turn off compression when you want to read command output yourself (debugging, reviewing logs). Turn it back on before starting an AI coding session.
docsCliReference.shellCompressionDesc: These commands run shell commands through lean-ctx's 90+ compression patterns. Output is automatically stripped of noise, boilerplate, and redundancy before reaching the LLM.
docsCliReference.shellDesc: Start an interactive shell session where all command output is compressed. Wraps your default shell (zsh, bash, or fish).
docsCliReference.slowLogDesc: Show or clear the slow command log at ~/.lean-ctx/slow-commands.log. Commands that take longer than expected are logged here for debugging.
docsCliReference.supportedLanguages: Supported languages for signatures mode: TypeScript, JavaScript, Rust, Python, Go, Java, C, C++, Ruby, C#, Kotlin, Swift, PHP (14 total via tree-sitter).
docsCliReference.teeDesc: Manage full output tee files saved to ~/.lean-ctx/tee/. Use lean-ctx tee last to view the most recent saved output. Configure via tee_mode: always, failures, or never.
docsCliReference.teeSubDesc: Manage error log files stored at ~/.lean-ctx/tee/. When tee_on_error is enabled in config, failed commands log their full output here.

docsCliReference.uninstallDesc: Remove all lean-ctx configuration: shell hooks, MCP configs from all editors, and the ~/.lean-ctx/ data directory. Prints instructions for removing the binary.

docsCliReference.wrappedDesc: Generate a shareable "Wrapped" report card of your token savings.
docsConfiguration.activeDesc: Recursion guard. When set, lean-ctx passes commands through without compression. Set automatically by the shell hook.
docsConfiguration.autonomyConfigDesc: The autonomous intelligence layer runs optimization pipelines automatically. Configure in ~/.lean-ctx/config.toml:
docsConfiguration.autonomyEnvDesc: Enable/disable autonomous intelligence features (auto-preload, auto-dedup, related hints). Default: true.
docsConfiguration.cacheTtlDesc: Seconds of inactivity before session cache auto-clears (0 = disabled)
docsConfiguration.checkpointIntervalDesc: Auto-checkpoint every N tool calls (0 = disabled). Overridden by LEAN_CTX_CHECKPOINT_INTERVAL env var.
docsConfiguration.checkpointIntervalEnvD: Auto-checkpoint every N tool calls (0 = disabled)
docsConfiguration.configFileDesc: LeanCTX supports an optional TOML configuration file at ~/.lean-ctx/config.toml.
docsConfiguration.crpModeDesc: Controls Token Dense Dialect compression level
docsConfiguration.cursorRuleDesc1: For maximum token savings, add a .cursor/rules/lean-ctx.mdc to your project. This instructs the LLM to prefer LeanCTX tools and use compact output patterns. An example is included in examples/lean-ctx.mdc in the repository.
docsConfiguration.cursorRuleDesc2: The Cursor rule includes CRP v2 (Compact Response Protocol) which can reduce both output tokens (50–80%) and thinking tokens (30–60%) through structured task parsing and one-hypothesis reasoning. These ranges are per-response estimates and depend on the model and task complexity.
docsConfiguration.description: Configuration options, environment variables, and editor setup.
docsConfiguration.disabledDesc: Master kill-switch. Bypasses ALL compression in both shell hook and MCP server. Everything passes through unmodified.
docsConfiguration.exitCodesDesc: In shell hook mode (-c), LeanCTX forwards the exit code of the wrapped command. This ensures scripts and CI pipelines work correctly. If the command fails (non-zero exit), the full original output is saved to ~/.lean-ctx/tee/ for debugging (when tee_mode is set to failures or always).
docsConfiguration.hookExcludeEnvDesc: Comma-separated glob patterns for hook redirect exclusion. Takes precedence over redirect_exclude in config.toml. Example: .wolf/**,.claude/**,*.json
docsConfiguration.hookRedirectDesc: The lean-ctx hook redirect command intercepts native Read/Grep/ListFiles tool calls and redirects them to lean-ctx MCP tools. To exclude specific paths from this redirect (e.g. for Edit workflows or framework protocol files), use the redirect_exclude config option or the LEAN_CTX_HOOK_EXCLUDE environment variable.
docsConfiguration.intro: LeanCTX works out of the box with zero configuration. All settings below are optional — defaults are tuned for typical AI-assisted development workflows.
docsConfiguration.passthroughUrlsDesc: URL fragments for curl passthrough. Curl commands targeting these URLs skip JSON compression and return full responses.
docsConfiguration.rawDesc: Skip compression for the current command. Same effect as --raw flag or raw=true MCP parameter.
docsConfiguration.redirectExcludeDesc: Paths excluded from the PreToolUse hook redirect. Matching files bypass the redirect and allow native Read/Grep/ListFiles. Supports glob patterns: *, ?, ** (recursive).
docsConfiguration.teeModeDesc: When to save full output to ~/.lean-ctx/tee/. Values: always, failures, never. Backward-compatible: tee_on_error: true maps to failures.
docsConfiguration.title: Configuration
docsConfiguration.tokenCountingDesc: lean-ctx uses tiktoken-rs with the o200k_base encoding (same as GPT-4o, Claude, and other modern models) for exact token counting. No estimation, no heuristics — every savings number you see in lean-ctx gain or the dashboard reflects real token counts.
docsConfiguration.outputDensityDesc: Controls global output verbosity for tool responses. Values: normal, terse, ultra. Can be overridden via LEAN_CTX_OUTPUT_DENSITY.
docsConfiguration.outputDensityEnvDesc: Override output density without editing config. Applies to all MCP tool outputs (and most tool footers).
docsConfiguration.modelsDirDesc: Override where embedding models are stored/downloaded (default: ~/.lean-ctx/models). Useful for containers and CI.
docsConfiguration.userTomlFiltersDesc: Define custom compression rules in ~/.lean-ctx/filters/*.toml. User filters are applied before builtin patterns.
docsConfiguration.userTomlFiltersManage: Manage via CLI: lean-ctx filter list, lean-ctx filter validate, lean-ctx filter init (creates example).
docsGettingStarted.agentRulesIntro: Even after MCP is configured, some AI agents may not automatically use lean-ctx's tools. They see the tools are available but default to their native equivalents (Read, Shell, Grep). To fix this, lean-ctx injects explicit usage rules into each tool's global configuration.

docsGettingStarted.intro: LeanCTX reduces your LLM's token consumption by up to 99% through a single Rust binary that provides: a Shell Hook with 90+ CLI compression patterns, an MCP Server with 34 tools for cached reads and session intelligence, and a Persistent Project Graph for structural code understanding. Install it once, and every file read, shell command, and code navigation becomes more efficient.

docsToolsIntelligence.intro: The Autonomous Intelligence Layer provides tools that go beyond basic compression — they understand your workflow, detect intent, deduplicate context, and intelligently manage token budgets. Since v2.14.1, these tools run autonomously: auto-preloading context on session start, appending related-file hints after reads, silently caching imported files, and deduplicating when the cache grows — all without explicit commands.
docsToolsIntelligence.title: Autonomous Intelligence Layer
docsToolsIntelligence.semanticSearchUpda: Hybrid semantic search using BM25 + dense embeddings with automatic incremental indexing. Falls back to BM25 when embeddings are unavailable.
docsToolsIntelligence.ctxSymbolDesc: Read a specific symbol (function/struct/class/method) by name and return only the relevant code span with line numbers.
docsToolsIntelligence.ctxOutlineDesc: Generate a compact outline of a file (symbols + signatures) without reading the full file content.
docsToolsIntelligence.ctxCallersDesc: Find which symbols call a given function/method using the persistent call graph (built incrementally).
docsToolsIntelligence.ctxCalleesDesc: List the functions/methods a given symbol calls using the persistent call graph (built incrementally).
docsToolsIntelligence.ctxRoutesDesc: Extract HTTP routes/endpoints from common frameworks and list them with handler and file location.
docsToolsIntelligence.ctxGraphDiagramDes: Generate Mermaid diagrams for dependency graphs (deps) or call graphs (calls).
docsToolsIntelligence.ctxCompressMemoryD: Compress memory/config markdown files (preserve code blocks, URLs, paths) and create a .original.md backup.
docsToolsMemory.description: ctx_knowledge, ctx_agent, ctx_overview — persistent project knowledge with temporal facts + contradiction detection, multi-agent coordination with persistent diaries, and task-aware project orientation with automatic wake-up briefing.
docsToolsMemory.intro: Four tools for cross-session memory, multi-agent coordination, and task-aware project orientation. ctx_knowledge now includes temporal fact tracking, automatic contradiction detection, cross-session search, and AAAK compact format. ctx_agent supports persistent agent diaries. ctx_overview provides automatic wake-up briefings.
docsToolsMemory.title: Memory & Multi-Agent

docsToolsSession.intro: These tools handle session persistence, performance monitoring, compression analysis, and benchmarking — everything you need to measure and optimize your token savings.

howItWorks.alg1Desc: BPE cross-entropy scoring — keeps tokens that surprise a language model, discards predictable boilerplate.
howItWorks.alg2Desc: PageRank + heat diffusion on the dependency graph — propagates importance through code relationships.
howItWorks.alg3Desc: Statistical mechanics for token budgets — distributes context capacity based on information temperature.
howItWorks.alg4Desc: Attention bridges identify natural break points in code — preserves coherent logical units, not arbitrary line counts.
howItWorks.alg5Desc: Jaccard similarity across cached files — eliminates redundant patterns without losing unique information.
howItWorks.alg6Desc: Token-aligned encoding — restructures output to minimize actual BPE token count, not just character count.
howItWorks.ctaDesc: One binary. Zero cloud dependencies. MIT licensed. Install in 60 seconds and start saving tokens immediately.
howItWorks.dataFlowDesc: lean-ctx intercepts at two levels: the context layer (file reads, code search, shell commands) and the shell layer (raw CLI output). Both compress data before it reaches the LLM. The AI sees the same information in a fraction of the tokens.
howItWorks.faq1A: lean-ctx is an open-source context engineering layer for AI coding tools. It sits between your AI tool (Cursor, Claude Code, GitHub Copilot, etc.) and the LLM, compressing context through AST-aware file compression, shell output pattern matching, and communication protocols. It reduces token usage by up to 99% per operation.
howItWorks.faq2A: No. lean-ctx uses tree-sitter AST parsing to understand code structure, then strips only what the LLM doesn't need: redundant whitespace, boilerplate imports, and decorative comments. On average, 98% of AST structure, 97% of identifiers, and 96% of meaningful code lines are preserved. You can verify this with lean-ctx benchmark run.
howItWorks.faq3A: Install via cargo install lean-ctx or curl -fsSL https://leanctx.com/install.sh | sh. Then run lean-ctx init for the shell hook and lean-ctx setup to connect with your AI tools. Zero configuration required.
howItWorks.faq4A: lean-ctx works with every major AI coding tool: Cursor, GitHub Copilot, Claude Code, Windsurf, Cline, OpenAI Codex, Gemini CLI, Antigravity, Pi, Crush, Zed, Continue, and OpenCode. All tools benefit from both the context server and shell hook compression.
howItWorks.faq4Q: Which AI tools does lean-ctx support?

howItWorks.flowYouDesc: Code normally. Change nothing about your workflow.
howItWorks.heroDesc: lean-ctx is a compression system with three independent layers. A context server replaces your AI's file reads with AST-aware compressed versions. A shell hook intercepts 90+ CLI command patterns. And three communication protocols teach the AI to respond more efficiently. You install it once — everything else is automatic.

manifest.abstractP1: In 2026, AI coding tools send full files, raw CLI output, and uncompressed project scans into context windows — every single time. The signal-to-noise ratio is abysmal. Based on tool-call analysis in multi-step coding sessions, ~65% of file reads are re-reads. Models waste attention on boilerplate that carries zero information entropy.
manifest.abstractP2: This paper argues that an Intelligence Layer — a transparent compression layer between the developer and the LLM — is the missing piece in the AI engineering stack. We present LeanCTX: a single Rust binary that achieves up to 99% per-operation token reduction (cache re-reads) while preserving all information the model needs to reason correctly.
manifest.abstractQuote: High performance with LLMs isn't about bigger context windows. It's about maximizing information entropy per token. LeanCTX is the intelligence buffer that ensures every token carries maximum signal.
manifest.archLayer1Desc: AST-based signatures via tree-sitter (18 languages), delta-loading for cached files, session caching with MD5 tracking, entropy filtering via Shannon analysis. Sends the skeleton, not the flesh. Re-reads cost 13 tokens instead of thousands.
manifest.archLayer2Desc: 6 read modes + line ranges let you choose the right fidelity per task. map mode for understanding, full mode for editing, signatures for API surface, entropy for noise filtering.
manifest.archLayer3Desc: Session cache with auto-TTL (5 min idle clear), context checkpoints via ctx_compress, subagent isolation with fresh=true. The model always sees the latest state, not the full history.
manifest.archLayer4Desc: Focused, high-entropy input means sharper reasoning. Less noise in the attention window = more attention on logic nodes = better code output. This is the emergent benefit of all other layers working together.
manifest.archP1: LeanCTX implements the Intelligence Layer as four composable layers. Each layer operates independently but compounds when used together.
manifest.archP2: The architecture is hybrid: a context server with 34 intelligent tools that replace editor built-ins (file reads, directory listings, code search, intent detection, project graphs), plus a transparent shell hook that compresses 90+ CLI patterns across 34 categories without changing your workflow.
manifest.conclusionCta: One Rust binary. Zero cloud dependencies. MIT licensed.
manifest.conclusionP1: Token limits, request quotas, and context window sizes define the AI coding landscape in 2026. The path forward isn't bigger context windows — it's making every token carry maximum information entropy.
manifest.conclusionP2: LeanCTX is a lossless minifier for human thought. It doesn't make the model see less. It makes the model see only what matters: the function signatures, the changed lines, the error codes, the dependency graph — stripped of the noise that dilutes reasoning.
manifest.conclusionQuote: 10K tokens of pure signal. That's the future of AI engineering.
manifest.densityHighlightSub: The goal of every Intelligence Layer interaction.
manifest.densityP1: A 200K-token context filled with boilerplate produces worse results than 10K tokens of pure signal. This isn't speculation — it's how attention mechanisms work. Every byte of noise stripped is a byte of reasoning capacity gained.
manifest.densityP2: Information entropy — measured in bits per token — is what determines whether a model reasons correctly. High-entropy tokens carry decisions, branching logic, API contracts, error handling. Low-entropy tokens carry whitespace, boilerplate, repetitive imports, and verbose CLI formatting.
manifest.densityP3: Consider a typical file re-read. The model already knows the file structure, the exports, the types. Sending 3,500 tokens of full source code when a 13-token cache confirmation suffices is a 99.6% waste of context capacity.
manifest.densityP4: The same logic applies to CLI output. npm install generates 800+ tokens of funding notices, deprecation warnings, and formatting. The information content? One line: package name, version, dependency count, timing.
manifest.description: A position paper on why information density — not context size — determines AI coding performance.
manifest.efficiencyP1: At 80% average compression — achievable with cached reads and shell hook combined — you don't save 80% of cost. You multiply capacity by 5x. Same budget, same subscription, five times the productive output.
manifest.efficiencyP2: This isn't about saving money — though it does that too. It's about making every interaction count. Longer sessions without context window resets. Deeper reasoning because the model isn't distracted by noise. Fewer failed completions because the relevant code is actually in the attention window.
manifest.efficiencyP3: The cost curve shifts from linear to logarithmic. Each additional token of context provides diminishing returns when it's noise, but compounding returns when it's signal.
manifest.efficiencyStat2Note: Typical session-wide average with caching + shell hook
manifest.heroDesc: A position paper on why information density — not context size — determines AI coding performance. By the LeanCTX team, March 2026.
manifest.principle1Desc: Every compression preserves the information the model needs. AST signatures keep function contracts intact. Diff mode shows exactly what changed. The filter never drops anything critical — every compression reverses cleanly at the semantic level.
manifest.principle2Desc: Every tool reports token counts. ctx_benchmark measures exact savings with tiktoken (o200k_base). ctx_metrics tracks cumulative stats. lean-ctx gain shows lifetime savings with USD cost estimates. You always know what's happening.
manifest.principle3Desc: Single Rust binary. No API keys, no accounts, no telemetry, no data collection. Your code never leaves your machine. MIT licensed, fully open source. Runs on macOS, Linux, and Windows with native binaries.
manifest.principle4Desc: 34 intelligent tools that each do one thing well. Use ctx_read for files, ctx_shell for CLI, ctx_compress for checkpoints. Mix and match for your workflow. Works with Cursor, GitHub Copilot, Claude Code, Windsurf, Crush, Codex, and more.
manifest.principle5Desc: All token counts use tiktoken with the o200k_base encoding — the same tokenizer the models use. No approximations, no heuristics. USD cost tracking with persistent lifetime statistics. Data-driven mode selection through ctx_analyze and ctx_benchmark.
manifest.principlesP1: Five principles guide every design decision in LeanCTX:
manifest.problemP1: We have models with million-token context windows and reasoning chains that span hundreds of steps. Yet most AI coding tools still send the full file on every read. That's like sending the entire library every time someone asks for a single page.
manifest.problemP2: The result: diluted attention, wasted compute, and reasoning that loses focus on the logic nodes that actually matter. Every redundant token competes with the actual signal in the attention mechanism — pushing the model's reasoning off the code paths that need analysis.
manifest.problemP3: Every AI tool has hard limits. 500 requests per day. 45 messages per 5 hours. 1,500 premium requests per month. Tokens are the new gold — but most tools burn them on boilerplate with zero information entropy.
manifest.problemP4: The problem isn't the model. It's the input.
manifest.problemStat1Note: Based on tool-call patterns in multi-step coding sessions
manifest.shiftP1: The old paradigm sends everything. The new paradigm sends only signal. Here's what changes when you introduce an Intelligence Layer:
manifest.shiftP2: The key insight: this isn't about seeing less. It's about seeing only what matters. The model receives the same logical information — function signatures, dependencies, changed lines, error messages — without the noise that dilutes its reasoning.

protocolsCcp.beginDesc: Highest attention weight. CCP places task description, architectural decisions, project topology, and file reference map here.
protocolsCcp.cacheClearDesc: Resets the entire file cache. Use when switching between unrelated tasks or when the cached state has diverged from disk. Session state is preserved — only file content cache is cleared.
protocolsCcp.cacheDesc: Stale cached data from a previous session can pollute a new conversation. CCP automatically clears the cache after inactivity, ensuring every new session starts clean.
protocolsCcp.cacheInvalidateDesc: Invalidate a single file's cache entry by path. Use after external edits (e.g., another tool modified the file) to ensure the next read reflects the current disk state.
protocolsCcp.ccpLine3: Task, findings, decisions, file refs — all intact
protocolsCcp.ccpLine4: ~400 tokens — picks up exactly where it left off
protocolsCcp.checkpointDesc: Long sessions accumulate context. Without intervention, the context window fills up and the AI loses track of earlier work. CCP prevents this automatically.
protocolsCcp.cleanBoundaryDesc: When you start a new chat, the AI gets fresh file reads instead of stale cached versions. Combined with session restore, this gives the best of both worlds — continuity without staleness.
protocolsCcp.cmdDecisionDesc: Record an architectural decision — prevents re-debating resolved choices
protocolsCcp.cmdFindingDesc: Record a key finding — bugs, patterns, important code locations
protocolsCcp.cmdLoadDesc: Restore previous session from disk — cross-chat memory in ~400 tokens
protocolsCcp.cmdSaveDesc: Force persist session state to disk — normally auto-saved
protocolsCcp.cmdStatusDesc: Show current session state — version, call count, active task, cached files
protocolsCcp.cmdTaskDesc: Set the current task — persisted across sessions for continuity
protocolsCcp.cmdWrappedDesc: Generate a savings report card — token savings, cache hit rates, compression stats
protocolsCcp.commandsDesc: CCP exposes a set of session management commands through the MCP server. The AI calls these automatically based on context — you can also invoke them manually.
protocolsCcp.configurableCacheDesc: Set LEAN_CTX_CACHE_TTL in seconds to adjust the idle timeout. Set to 0 to disable automatic cache clearing entirely.
protocolsCcp.configurableDesc: Set LEAN_CTX_CHECKPOINT_INTERVAL to adjust the checkpoint frequency. Lower values create more frequent checkpoints for very long sessions. Higher values reduce overhead for shorter workflows.
protocolsCcp.ctaDesc: CCP is enabled by default with zero configuration. Install lean-ctx, run init, and your AI remembers context across every session.
protocolsCcp.demoDesc: This is what happens when you start a new chat. The AI calls ctx_session load and instantly has full project context — task, findings, decisions, and file references.
protocolsCcp.description: CCP gives your AI memory that persists across chat sessions. Restore previous session state in ~400 tokens instead of re-reading 50,000 tokens of context.
protocolsCcp.endDesc: Second-highest attention. CCP places recent findings, test results, and next steps here for immediate relevance.
protocolsCcp.every15Desc: lean-ctx automatically creates a compressed checkpoint combined with the current session state. The checkpoint captures task, findings, decisions, and file references in a compact format.
protocolsCcp.fiveMinDesc: After 5 minutes of inactivity — triggered by a new chat or context compaction — the file cache auto-clears. This prevents outdated file contents from leaking into new sessions.
protocolsCcp.freshTrueDesc: Pass fresh=true to any ctx_read call to bypass the cache entirely. The file is read from disk and the result is not cached. Use when spawned as a subagent or after context compaction.
protocolsCcp.heroDesc: CCP gives your AI memory that persists across chat sessions and context compactions. Instead of re-reading 50,000 tokens of context on every new conversation, CCP restores the previous session state in approximately 400 tokens.
protocolsCcp.isolationDesc: Modern AI tools spawn subagents — independent workers that run in parallel. Each subagent needs its own fresh context, not the parent agent's cached state. CCP provides mechanisms to prevent cache leakage.
protocolsCcp.keepsCompactDesc: Each checkpoint compresses the accumulated context, preventing the window from bloating. The AI retains all critical information while discarding redundant intermediate states.
protocolsCcp.litmDesc: LLMs do not attend equally to all tokens. Research by Liu et al. (2023) showed that models pay significantly more attention to tokens at the beginning and end of the context window, while middle tokens get "lost" with lower attention weights. CCP exploits this directly.
protocolsCcp.middleDesc: Lowest attention weight — the "lost in the middle" zone. Historical context and lower-priority information goes here.
protocolsCcp.problemDesc: Every new chat session starts with zero context. The AI knows nothing about your project, your architecture, your recent changes, or what you were working on five minutes ago.
protocolsCcp.sessionPreservedDesc: Before clearing the cache, CCP auto-saves the session state to disk. The task, findings, decisions, and file reference map survive the cache clear and can be restored with ctx_session load.
protocolsCcp.title: Context Continuity Protocol (CCP) — LeanCTX
protocolsCcp.tradLine5: Slow start, repeated questions, lost decisions
protocolsCcp.zeroInterventionDesc: Checkpoints happen transparently. The AI doesn't pause, doesn't ask permission, and doesn't interrupt your workflow. The compressed state is appended to the response automatically.
protocolsCep.architecturalDesc: Design decisions, full analysis. Edge cases and trade-offs must be considered.
protocolsCep.code1LINE: One line per action. Summarize, don't explain
protocolsCep.codeABBREV: Use abbreviations: fn, cfg, impl, deps, req, res, ctx, err
protocolsCep.codeACT1: Act immediately, report result in one line
protocolsCep.codeACTFIRST: Execute tool calls immediately. Never narrate before acting
protocolsCep.codeBRIEF: Summarize approach in 1–2 lines, then act
protocolsCep.codeDELTA: Only show changed lines, not full files
protocolsCep.codeDIFF: For code changes: show only diff lines, not full files
protocolsCep.codeFREF: Reference files by Fn refs only, never full paths
protocolsCep.codeFULL: Outline approach, consider edge cases, then act
protocolsCep.codeNODOC: Don't add comments that narrate what code does
protocolsCep.codeNOMOCK: Never use mock data, fake values, or placeholder code
protocolsCep.codeNOREPEAT: Never repeat known context. Reference cached files by Fn ID
protocolsCep.codeQUALITY: Never skip edge case analysis or error handling to save tokens
protocolsCep.complexityDesc: CEP adapts its instruction set based on task complexity. Simple tasks get minimal instructions for maximum speed. Complex tasks get full analysis with quality anchoring.
protocolsCep.ctaDesc: Install lean-ctx, run init, and CEP starts scoring from the first interaction. No configuration, no flags, no env vars. It just works.
protocolsCep.demoDesc: CEP scores are appended to tool responses automatically. The AI sees its own efficiency metrics and adjusts behavior accordingly.
protocolsCep.demoTitle: What a CEP score looks like.
protocolsCep.description: CEP scores every AI response on a 0-100 scale across four dimensions. It teaches the AI to act first, report concisely, and adapt verbosity to task complexity.
protocolsCep.dimActionDesc: Whether the AI acts first and reports concisely, or narrates its plan before acting. Action-first communication eliminates wasted preamble tokens.
protocolsCep.dimCacheDesc: How often the AI reuses cached file reads instead of re-reading the same file. Higher cache hit rates mean fewer redundant tokens flowing through the context window.
protocolsCep.dimCompressDesc: The ratio of compressed vs raw tokens in the context. Measures how effectively the AI leverages lean-ctx's compression modes rather than requesting uncompressed data.
protocolsCep.dimModesDesc: How well the AI picks the right read mode for each task. Using map for context-only files and full for editable files shows efficient mode selection.
protocolsCep.heroDesc: CEP scores every AI response on a 0–100 scale across four dimensions. It teaches the AI to act first, report concisely, and adapt its verbosity to the complexity of the task at hand.
protocolsCep.mechanicalDesc: Simple fixes, direct execution. No reasoning overhead needed — just do it.
protocolsCep.refDesc: Each code is a single keyword that tells the AI how to behave. CEP combines them based on task complexity. They can also be used manually in your prompts.
protocolsCep.rule1Desc: Execute tool calls immediately. Never narrate before acting. The AI should show results, not announce intentions.
protocolsCep.rule2Desc: Never repeat known context. Reference cached files by Fn ID. If the AI already read a file, it should reference F3:42, not quote the content again.
protocolsCep.rule3Desc: Use notation, not sentences. Changes: +line / -line / ~line (modified). Status: tool(args) → result.
protocolsCep.rule4Desc: Summarize, don't explain. Every action gets one line of output. No multi-paragraph descriptions of what was done.
protocolsCep.rule5Desc: Never skip edge case analysis or error handling to save tokens. Complex tasks require full reasoning. Only reduce prose, never reduce thinking.
protocolsCep.rulesDesc: These rules govern how the AI structures every response. They are non-negotiable — CEP enforces them regardless of complexity level.
protocolsCep.scoringDesc: Each dimension measures a distinct aspect of communication efficiency. They are scored independently, then combined into the final CEP score.
protocolsCep.standardDesc: Multi-file changes, reasoning needed. Brief explanation before acting.
protocolsCep.step1Desc: CEP instructions are injected automatically via lean-ctx's MCP server. No manual setup required.
protocolsCep.step2Desc: Targets the AI's response tokens — not input. The AI learns to communicate more efficiently.
protocolsCep.step3Desc: Cache hit rate, read mode diversity, compression ratio, and action-first communication are scored independently.
protocolsCep.step4Desc: Dimensions combine into a 0–100 score. Higher means the AI is wasting fewer tokens communicating.
protocolsCep.title: CEP — Cognitive Efficiency Protocol — LeanCTX
protocolsCep.whatDesc: CEP is injected via the system prompt. It does not touch the data flowing into the AI — that is the MCP server's job. Instead, CEP optimizes the AI's output tokens: how it communicates back. Four scoring dimensions are evaluated independently and combined into a single 0–100 score.
protocolsCep.whatTitle: An instruction protocol that optimizes AI output.
protocolsIndex.autoDesc: All three protocols are enabled by default with zero configuration. lean-ctx injects them via system prompt instructions — the AI follows them automatically. No flags, no env vars, no config files.
protocolsIndex.ccpDesc: CCP gives your AI memory that persists across chat sessions. Instead of re-reading 50,000 tokens of context on every new conversation, CCP restores the previous session state in ~400 tokens. The AI picks up exactly where it left off.
protocolsIndex.cepDesc: CEP scores every AI response on a 0–100 scale across four dimensions: cache hit rate, read mode diversity, compression ratio, and action-first communication. It teaches the AI to adapt its verbosity to the complexity of the task — from mechanical fixes to architectural decisions.
protocolsIndex.ctaDesc: All three protocols activate automatically after setup. Your AI communicates more concisely from the very first message.
protocolsIndex.description: CEP (efficiency scoring), CCP (cross-session memory), and TDD (symbol shorthand) — three protocols that optimize how AI communicates, remembers context, and minimizes token waste. Zero configuration, enabled by default.
protocolsIndex.heroDesc: lean-ctx doesn't just compress data — it reduces noise in how your AI communicates, restores session context, and cuts response verbosity. Three protocols work together to eliminate wasted tokens at every layer of communication. All three are enabled by default with zero configuration.
protocolsIndex.tddDesc: TDD replaces verbose prose with symbolic notation. Every token carries meaning — no articles, no filler, no pleasantries. The AI communicates in a compact dialect that preserves all information with 8–25% fewer tokens.
protocolsIndex.title: Protocols — LeanCTX
protocolsTdd.comparisonDesc: The same information, expressed in standard prose versus TDD. The dense version conveys identical meaning in 71% fewer tokens.
protocolsTdd.ctaDesc: TDD activates automatically with lean-ctx. No configuration, no flags. Install once and your AI communicates at maximum density from the first message.
protocolsTdd.description: TDD replaces verbose prose with symbolic notation. Maximize meaning per token — no articles, no filler, no pleasantries. 8-25% fewer tokens with minimal information loss.
protocolsTdd.fileRefDesc: Every file the AI reads gets a short identifier that persists across the entire session. Combined with session caching, re-reads cost a fraction of the original read.
protocolsTdd.fileRefIds: Files get short IDs on first read. No more repeating full paths.
protocolsTdd.fileRefMap: Maps long identifiers to short IDs. AuthService becomes A1.
protocolsTdd.fileRefReread: Re-reads cost approximately 13 tokens thanks to session caching.
protocolsTdd.fileRefSession: IDs persist across the entire session. F1 always refers to the same file.
protocolsTdd.heroDesc: TDD replaces verbose prose with symbolic notation. Maximize meaning per token — no articles, no filler, no pleasantries. The AI communicates in a compact dialect that preserves all information with 8–25% fewer tokens.
protocolsTdd.highLine2: Conveys the same meaning in less space
protocolsTdd.highLine3: Frees token budget for actual logic and context
protocolsTdd.lowLine3: The AI already knows it's a function from context
protocolsTdd.lowLine4: Wastes budget on what's already implied
protocolsTdd.notationDesc: TDD defines a compact notation for describing code changes. Instead of verbose descriptions, the AI reports modifications as structured one-liners.
protocolsTdd.problemDesc: Information entropy per token is the key metric. Most AI responses are filled with tokens that carry nearly zero unique information.
protocolsTdd.problemFootnote: lean-ctx is a lossless minifier for human thought. TDD applies this principle to AI communication — stripping syntactic sugar while preserving every bit of semantic content.
protocolsTdd.rule1Desc: Remove articles (a, the, an), filler words, and pleasantries. No "I've successfully..." or "Let me help you with..."
protocolsTdd.rule2Desc: Reference files by Fn refs (F1, F2, F3), never full paths. Short IDs persist across the session.
protocolsTdd.rule3Desc: For code changes, show only the changed lines — not the full file. Use change notation with symbols.
protocolsTdd.rule4Desc: No explanations unless asked. Execute first, report concisely. The code is the explanation.
protocolsTdd.rule5Desc: Use tables for structured data. Denser than prose, faster to scan, fewer tokens to express relationships.
protocolsTdd.rule6Desc: fn, cfg, impl, deps, req, res, ctx, err, ok, ret, arg, val, ty, mod
protocolsTdd.rulesDesc: TDD enforces six rules that the AI follows in every response. These rules compound — each one reduces token count while preserving the information the developer actually needs.
protocolsTdd.symbolDesc: Each symbol replaces 1 token of prose with 1 token of dense notation. The AI learns these symbols from the system prompt and uses them consistently throughout the session.
protocolsTdd.title: Token Dense Dialect (TDD) — LeanCTX
shellHook.categoriesDesc: lean-ctx recognizes output patterns from all major developer tools and compresses each one with purpose-built rules.
shellHook.ctaDesc: One setup command enables the shell hook for your shell. Every AI command thereafter is automatically compressed.
shellHook.flowDesc: The shell hook wraps your commands transparently. The AI sees compressed output — you see nothing different.
shellHook.heroDesc: The shell hook intercepts CLI output before your AI sees it. Git diffs, npm installs, cargo builds, docker logs — all compressed to their essential information. No configuration, no aliases to learn.
shellHook.metaDescription: lean-ctx shell hook intercepts CLI output from 90+ command patterns across 34+ categories — git, npm, cargo, docker, kubectl and more — compressing output transparently.
shellHook.rawModeDesc: When you need uncompressed output, bypass compression with a single flag. No more silent data loss — truncation is always transparent.
shellHook.title: Shell Hook — LeanCTX
shellHook.worksIndependentlyDesc: The shell hook operates independently of the context server. AI tools that haven't been configured yet still benefit from compressed shell output. The hook intercepts at the shell level, so every tool that runs commands gets savings.
terminal.toolsConfigured: AI tools configured, shell hook active
testimonials.daniel: I was skeptical about the 99% claim. Then I ran ctx_read in map mode on a 400-line service file and got back 30 tokens. That's not marketing — that's math.
testimonials.erik: Finally someone built what Cursor should have built-in. Session caching alone — re-reads cost 13 tokens instead of thousands.
testimonials.jay: Been using RTK before. lean-ctx is a completely different level — it doesn't just compress output, it understands code structure. The AST modes are insane.
testimonials.marco: lean-ctx gain shows I've saved $48 in one week. That's real money. And the AI responses are actually better because it gets cleaner context.
testimonials.radmar: The shell hook saves hundreds of thousands of tokens per day. git log, cargo test, npm run — everything compressed automatically. Zero config.
testimonials.sebastian: I installed lean-ctx and my Cursor sessions immediately felt snappier. Files that used to eat 2K tokens now cost 50.
docsRemoteSetup.title: Remote Setup
docsRemoteSetup.description: Run lean-ctx on remote servers, containers, and headless environments using SSH tunnels.
docsRemoteSetup.intro: lean-ctx runs as a stdio MCP server — your AI client spawns it as a local subprocess. For remote servers, containers, or headless environments, you can tunnel the connection over SSH. No additional server component needed.
docsRemoteSetup.sshTunnelDesc: The simplest approach: configure your AI client to run lean-ctx via SSH. The MCP protocol flows over SSH's stdin/stdout — no ports to open, no HTTP, no authentication to manage. SSH handles encryption and authentication natively.
docsRemoteSetup.sshStep1Title: 1. Install lean-ctx on the remote server

docsRemoteSetup.sshStep3Desc: Point your AI client's MCP config to run lean-ctx via SSH. This works with Cursor, Claude Code, Windsurf, and any MCP-compatible client:
docsRemoteSetup.sshStep3Note: This tells the client to spawn ssh user@server "lean-ctx" instead of a local lean-ctx. The MCP protocol flows transparently over the SSH connection — stdin/stdout is tunneled automatically.
docsRemoteSetup.portForwardingDesc: If you want to access the lean-ctx dashboard (Observatory) from your local browser while lean-ctx runs on a remote server, use SSH port forwarding:
docsRemoteSetup.persistentTunnelDesc: Then connect and start the dashboard:

docsIdeSetup.description: Make lean-ctx work reliably across IDEs and AI coding tools.

docsIdeSetup.oneShotNote: After configuring an IDE, restart the IDE completely so it reconnects to MCP. MCP changes do not apply to an already running process.
